title: Math
intro: Questions on the Math unit draw heavily on the standard undergraduate pure-math sequence, with linear algebra and real analysis supplying the single most frequent answerlines. Terms like polynomials, eigenvalues, determinant, continuity, integration, and differentiation appear constantly, alongside the machinery built on top of them: Taylor and Fourier series, Laplace and Fourier transforms, and convergence tests. Topology contributes a dense secondary cluster — compactness, connectedness, manifolds, knots, and the Poincare conjecture — reflecting how often quizbowl draws on the qualitative, definition-heavy side of the field.

Number theory and abstract algebra form the next major clusters. Prime numbers, Fermat's Last Theorem, elliptic curves, and Diophantine equations recur as both standalone answers and as context clues pointing to the mathematicians who worked on them, while groups, rings, fields, and Galois theory anchor the algebraic side, tying together figures from Galois and Abel through Noether and Grothendieck. Probability and statistics form their own coherent bloc — the normal distribution, variance, ANOVA, regression, and hypothesis-testing machinery like the p-value and central limit theorem — distinct in flavor from the pure-math canon but just as frequently tossed up.

A large share of answerlines are historical figures rather than concepts: Euler, Gauss, Cauchy, Hilbert, Riemann, Erdos, and Ramanujan recur across nearly every subfield, often as common-link answers tying together a cluster of theorems or objects that bear their names. Foundational topics — the continuum hypothesis, the axiom of choice, Godel's incompleteness theorems, and Cantor's set theory — form a smaller but conceptually distinct cluster probing the logical underpinnings of the subject. Finally, specific numbers and constants (pi, the golden ratio, and small integers like four or twelve) appear regularly as answerlines in their own right, usually keyed to a distinguishing mathematical property rather than pure trivia.

sections.name: Foundations & Logic
sections.blurb: Set theory, axioms, and the logical bedrock underlying all of mathematics.

entries.note: German mathematician who posed 23 problems in 1900; foundational work on Hilbert spaces and formalism in logic.
Claims no set has cardinality strictly between the integers and the reals; shown independent of ZFC by Godel and Cohen.
Collections of distinct objects; the basic object of set theory, described via the Zermelo-Fraenkel axioms.
Founder of set theory; proved the reals are uncountable via diagonalization and introduced transfinite cardinal numbers.
Set-theoretic axiom that any collection of nonempty sets has a choice function; independent of ZF, implies the Banach-Tarski paradox.
Proof technique establishing a base case, then a step showing truth for n implies truth for n+1.
The unique set containing no elements, a subset of every set.
Logician whose incompleteness theorems show any consistent formal system contains truths it cannot prove.
Fractal subset of [0,1] formed by repeatedly removing middle thirds; uncountable yet has measure zero.
Set of all ordered pairs, or tuples, formed from two or more sets.
Describes a set or quantity with a limited, countable number of elements.
Technique developed by Paul Cohen to prove independence results in set theory, e.g. of the continuum hypothesis.
Hungarian-American polymath with contributions to set theory, game theory, and computer architecture.
States a solid ball can be decomposed and reassembled into two balls identical to the original, using the axiom of choice.
Smallest closed set containing a given set, or the property that an operation stays within a set.
Describes a set whose elements can be put into one-to-one correspondence with the natural numbers.
Describes a set or quantity without bound, explored via Cantor's theory of cardinalities.
Set of elements common to two or more sets.
Set of all subsets of a given set, including the empty set and the set itself.
States every nonempty set of positive integers has a least element; equivalent to induction.

sections.name: Number Theory
sections.blurb: The study of integers, primes, and the equations and conjectures built from them.

entries.note: Integers greater than 1 divisible only by 1 and themselves; the building blocks of arithmetic via unique factorization.
"Prince of mathematicians"; wrote Disquisitiones Arithmeticae, contributed to the normal distribution and Gaussian elimination.
States no positive integers a,b,c satisfy a^n+b^n=c^n for n>2; conjectured in 1637, proved by Andrew Wiles in 1994.
Numbers expressible as a ratio of integers, denoted Q; a countable set contrasted with the irrationals.
The set of whole numbers including negatives and zero, denoted Z.
17th-century French lawyer-mathematician; namesake of Fermat's Last Theorem, Fermat's little theorem, and early probability work.
The complete ordered field including rationals and irrationals, denoted R.
Complex function whose nontrivial zeros are conjectured, per the Riemann hypothesis, to have real part 1/2.
Curves of form y^2=x^3+ax+b; central to the proof of Fermat's Last Theorem and to modern cryptography.
Gives a unique solution modulo the product of pairwise coprime moduli for a system of simultaneous congruences.
Numbers of form a+bi with i=sqrt(-1); form an algebraically closed field, denoted C.
Self-taught Indian mathematician, prolific in number theory and infinite series; mentored by G.H. Hardy.
Diophantine equation x^2-Dy^2=1 with integer solutions found via continued fractions.
Conjecture that all nontrivial zeros of the Riemann zeta function have real part 1/2; an unsolved Millennium Prize problem.
Polynomial equations for which only integer solutions are sought, named for Diophantus.
Gauss's theorem relating the solvability of quadratic congruences modulo two odd primes.
The positive integers, sometimes including zero, used for counting.
Number system extending the rationals using a notion of distance based on divisibility by a prime p.
An integer greater than 1 with no positive divisors other than 1 and itself.
States that for coprime integers a+b=c, the product of distinct prime factors of abc is rarely much smaller than c.
Sequences with a constant difference between consecutive terms.
Counts positive integers up to n coprime to n; central to RSA cryptography.
Counts positive integers up to n coprime to n; central to RSA cryptography.
States every integer greater than 1 has a unique factorization into primes.
Largest positive integer dividing two or more integers, found via the Euclidean algorithm.
Primes of the form 2^p-1, searched for via distributed computing projects like GIMPS.
Positive integers equal to the sum of their proper divisors, e.g. 6 and 28.
Refers to the real numbers, the complete ordered field including rationals and irrationals.
Branch of mathematics dealing with numbers and basic operations; also used for number-theoretic study of integers.
7th-century Indian mathematician who formalized rules for zero and negative numbers and gave a formula for cyclic quadrilateral area.
A positive integer with more than two divisors, i.e. not prime.
Expressions representing numbers as nested fractions, used to approximate irrationals.
German mathematician behind Dirichlet's theorem on primes in arithmetic progressions and the modern definition of a function.
Expression of an integer as a product of prime numbers.
Integers greater than 1 divisible only by 1 and themselves.
Ancient algorithm for finding all primes up to a limit by iteratively marking multiples of each prime.
Pairs of primes differing by two, e.g. 11 and 13; conjectured to be infinite in number.

sections.name: Algebra
sections.blurb: Groups, rings, fields, and the structures and symmetries that generalize arithmetic.

entries.note: Algebraic expressions of terms with variables raised to non-negative integer powers; central to factoring, roots, and interpolation.
Scalars satisfying Av=v for a matrix and eigenvector; found via the characteristic polynomial, central to diagonalization.
Scalar computed from a square matrix encoding volume scaling and invertibility; zero determinant means the matrix is singular.
Sets with an associative binary operation, identity, and inverses; the basic object of abstract algebra encoding symmetry.
Property that order of operation doesn't matter (ab=ba); defines commutative rings and abelian groups.
Sum of a square matrix's diagonal entries, equal to the sum of its eigenvalues.
Property that a binary operation's order doesn't affect the result (ab=ba).
Set of elements mapped to the identity or zero by a homomorphism; measures the map's failure to be injective.
Property of vectors or functions with zero inner product; basis of orthogonal bases and Fourier analysis.
Rectangular arrays of numbers representing linear transformations, manipulated via addition, multiplication, and inversion.
Norwegian mathematician who proved the general quintic has no algebraic solution; namesake of abelian groups.
Describes vectors with zero inner product, or matrices whose columns form an orthonormal basis.
Set of eigenvalues of a linear operator or matrix, generalized in operator theory.
Algebraic structures with two operations, addition and multiplication, generalizing the integers.
Branch studying mathematical structures via objects and morphisms, abstracting common patterns across fields.
Polynomial det(A- I) whose roots are a matrix's eigenvalues.
Operation gag^-1 in group theory, or the complex conjugate a-bi of a+bi.
Algebraic structures where addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division (except by zero) are defined, e.g. Q, R, C.
Partially ordered sets where every pair of elements has a unique supremum and infimum.
Rectangular array of numbers representing a linear transformation.
Describes a polynomial or equation of degree two, solved via the quadratic formula.
Dimension of a matrix's column or row space, i.e. the number of linearly independent rows or columns.
Solutions to an equation where a function equals zero.
Quantities with magnitude and direction, elements of a vector space combined via addition and scalar multiplication.
20th-century French-German mathematician who reshaped algebraic geometry via scheme theory and category theory.
Property that grouping of operations doesn't change the result: (ab)c=a(bc).
Set of linearly independent vectors that span a vector space.
French mathematician; namesake of the Jordan normal form, Jordan curve theorem, and Jordan-Holder theorem.
States the absolute value of an inner product of two vectors is at most the product of their norms.
Entries of a matrix running from top-left to bottom-right, or Cantor's diagonal argument technique.
French mathematician who founded Galois theory linking field extensions to group theory, showing quintics unsolvable by radicals; died in a duel at 20.
Ways of expressing group elements as matrices acting on a vector space.
Describes equations or functions of degree one, or maps preserving addition and scalar multiplication.
Four-dimensional number system extending the complex numbers, discovered by Hamilton, with noncommutative multiplication.
Complex numbers that yield 1 when raised to a positive integer power; vertices of a regular polygon on the unit circle.
Describes a matrix equal to its transpose, or an object invariant under some transformation.
Sets of vectors closed under addition and scalar multiplication, satisfying field-like axioms.
Describes a group whose operation is commutative, named for Niels Abel.
Binary operation on two vectors in three dimensions yielding a vector perpendicular to both.
Scalar such that Av=v for some nonzero vector v and matrix A.
Special subsets of a ring closed under addition and absorption of multiplication by ring elements.
Square matrix with ones on the diagonal and zeros elsewhere, acting as the multiplicative identity.
Describes a function that maps distinct inputs to distinct outputs, i.e. one-to-one.
Element or function that reverses another's effect, e.g. a matrix's inverse or a function's inverse.
Linear map sending a vector space onto a subspace, idempotent when applied twice.
Multilinear generalizations of scalars, vectors, and matrices used in physics and differential geometry.
Describes numbers or expressions that are roots of polynomial equations with rational coefficients.
British mathematician and matrix algebra pioneer; namesake of the Cayley-Hamilton theorem and Cayley graphs.
Set of linearly independent vectors spanning a vector space.
Subgroup of elements that commute with a given element or subset of a group.
Smallest number of times one must add a ring's multiplicative identity to get zero, or zero if this never happens.
Refers to results like the classification of finite simple groups, organizing structures into complete lists.
Complex number a-bi paired with a+bi, or elements related by gag^-1 in group theory.
Describes a group generated by a single element.
Highest power of a variable in a polynomial, or the number of edges meeting at a graph vertex.
Operation on two vectors yielding a scalar, equal to the sum of products of corresponding components.
Vector space of linear functionals mapping a given vector space to its underlying field.
20th-century mathematician who contributed to class field theory and Artin's reciprocity law and co-developed modern algebra.
German mathematician with foundational work on Noetherian rings and Noether's theorem linking symmetries to conservation laws.
Algebraic structure with addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division, except by zero.
States every non-constant polynomial with complex coefficients has at least one complex root.
Evariste Galois, French mathematician who linked polynomial solvability to group theory.
Connects field extensions to group theory, explaining why quintic equations have no general radical solution.
Algorithm converting a set of vectors into an orthogonal or orthonormal basis.
Structure-preserving map between two algebraic objects of the same type.
Element that leaves other elements unchanged under a given operation, e.g. 0 for addition or 1 for multiplication.
Describes a matrix or function that has an inverse.
Describes a polynomial that cannot be factored into lower-degree polynomials over a given field.
States composition series of a finite group are unique up to permutation and isomorphism.
States the order of a subgroup divides the order of its finite parent group.
Factoring a matrix into a product of simpler matrices, e.g. LU, QR, or singular value decomposition.
Norwegian mathematician who proved the general quintic is unsolvable by radicals; namesake of abelian groups.
Function assigning a nonnegative length or size to vectors in a vector space.
Subgroups invariant under conjugation by any element of the parent group; the kernels of homomorphisms.
Describes a matrix whose associated quadratic form is always positive for nonzero vectors.
Ideals of a ring analogous to prime numbers, whose quotient rings are integral domains.
Result of multiplying two or more quantities.
States homomorphisms between irreducible representations are either zero or isomorphisms.
Describes a group with no nontrivial normal subgroups, the building blocks of finite group classification.
Nonnegative square roots of eigenvalues of A*A, used in the singular value decomposition of a matrix.
Norwegian mathematician and founder of Lie groups and Lie algebras describing continuous symmetry.
Describes a matrix or data structure with mostly zero or absent entries.
The 26 finite simple groups that don't fit into the standard infinite families, including the Monster group.
Results guaranteeing the existence and properties of subgroups of prime-power order within a finite group.
Groups of all permutations of a finite set.
Property of an object invariant under some transformation, studied via group theory.
Irish mathematician who discovered quaternions and formulated Hamiltonian mechanics.

sections.name: Analysis & Calculus
sections.blurb: Limits, derivatives, integrals, series, and the machinery of continuous change.

entries.note: French mathematician who rigorized analysis; namesake of Cauchy sequences, the Cauchy-Riemann equations, and Cauchy's integral formula.
Property of a function whose small input changes produce small output changes; formalized via epsilon-delta limits.
Operation finding area under a curve or an antiderivative; inverse of differentiation via the fundamental theorem of calculus.
Estimating values between known data points, e.g. via Lagrange polynomials or splines.
Prolific 18th-century Swiss mathematician; introduced e and Euler's formula/identity, and founded graph theory via Konigsberg's bridges.
Decomposes a function into constituent frequencies; foundational to signal processing and partial differential equations.
Property of a sequence or series approaching a limit; assessed via tests like the ratio and root tests.
Operation finding a function's instantaneous rate of change; inverse of integration.
Represents a function as an infinite sum of terms built from its derivatives at a point.
Vector of partial derivatives pointing in the direction of steepest increase of a multivariable function.
"Father of modern analysis" who rigorized epsilon-delta calculus; gave an everywhere-continuous, nowhere-differentiable function.
Operation combining two functions to produce a third; central to signal processing and sums of independent random variables.
Generalized function that is zero everywhere except the origin and integrates to one; used to model an impulse.
Integral transform converting functions of time into functions of complex frequency, used to solve differential equations.
The instantaneous rate of change of a function; the slope of its tangent line.
Points mapped to themselves by a function; guaranteed to exist under conditions like Brouwer's fixed-point theorem.
Approximating definite integrals via methods like the trapezoidal rule or Simpson's rule.
Property of a metric space in which every Cauchy sequence converges within the space.
Describes a function that possesses a derivative at a point or throughout its domain.
Equations relating a function to its derivatives, used to model change over time as ODEs and PDEs.
Function of form e^x (or a^x) characterized by a growth rate proportional to its own value.
Representation of a periodic function as a sum of sines and cosines.
Inverse of exponentiation; log base b of x gives the power to which b must be raised to yield x.
Infinite series of terms involving increasing powers of a variable, e.g. Taylor or Maclaurin series.
States the length of one side of a triangle, or the norm of a sum, cannot exceed the sum of the other two.
Describes a set or function confined within some finite range.
German mathematician known for elliptic functions and the Jacobian matrix and determinant.
Property of a function with no abrupt jumps or breaks, formalized via limits.
Extends the factorial to complex numbers.
18th-century mathematician behind Lagrangian mechanics, Lagrange multipliers, and Lagrange's theorem in group theory.
Method for finding extrema of a function subject to equality constraints.
Generalization of length, area, and volume assigning size to subsets of a space; foundation of Lebesgue integration.
Set functions generalizing length, area, and volume; the foundation of measure theory.
Technique extending the domain of an analytic function beyond its original definition.
Solutions to Bessel's differential equation, arising in wave and heat problems with cylindrical symmetry.
States every bounded sequence in R^n has a convergent subsequence.
Trigonometric function giving the x-coordinate of a point on the unit circle at a given angle.
A point mapped to itself by a function, e.g. via Brouwer's or Banach's fixed-point theorems.
Describes a complex function that is complex-differentiable in a neighborhood of every point in its domain.
Value representing accumulated area under a curve; the antiderivative in indefinite form.
States a continuous function on [a,b] takes every value between f(a) and f(b).
Co-inventor of calculus with Leibniz; developed the fundamental theorem of calculus and Newton's method.
Logarithm base e, denoted ln(x); the inverse of the exponential function e^x.
Describes a differential equation splittable into functions of each variable, or a space with a countable dense subset.
Trigonometric function giving the y-coordinate of a point on the unit circle at a given angle.
Trigonometric function equal to sine over cosine, or a line touching a curve at one point.
Describes a function locally given by a convergent power series.
Describes a sequence or series that approaches a finite limit.
Vector operator measuring a vector field's outward flux density at a point.
Describes growth proportional to current value, as in functions of form e^x.
Refers to the gamma function extending the factorial, or the Euler-Mascheroni constant.
Refers to the divergent harmonic series of reciprocals, or harmonic functions satisfying Laplace's equation.
States a subset of Euclidean space is compact if and only if it is closed and bounded.
Matrix of second-order partial derivatives, used to classify critical points as maxima, minima, or saddles.
Complex functions differentiable at every point in an open set.
Technique for integrating products of functions, derived from the product rule.
Matrix of all first-order partial derivatives of a vector-valued function, used in changes of variables.
S-shaped curve modeling bounded growth, used in logistic regression and population models.
Equations involving partial derivatives of a multivariable function, modeling heat, waves, and fields.
Coefficient of the (z-a)^-1 term in a complex function's Laurent series, used in contour integration.
Critical points that are neither a local maximum nor minimum, curving up in one direction and down in another.
The sum of the terms of a sequence, convergent or divergent.
Describes a function possessing continuous derivatives up to some order.
Polish mathematician and founder of functional analysis; namesake of Banach spaces and the Banach-Tarski paradox.
Generalizes the fundamental theorem of calculus, relating a surface integral of a curl to a line integral around its boundary.
Localized oscillating functions used to decompose signals at multiple resolutions.
Property of a set or function confined within finite limits.
Differentiation rule for composite functions: the derivative of f(g(x)) is f'(g(x))g'(x).
Study of dynamical systems highly sensitive to initial conditions.
Sequences of orthogonal polynomials used in approximation theory and numerical analysis.
Describes a metric space in which every Cauchy sequence converges within the space.
Branch of mathematics studying functions of complex variables and their differentiability.
Points where a function's derivative is zero or undefined, candidates for extrema.
Vector operator measuring a vector field's rotation or circulation at a point.
Iterative optimization algorithm moving against a function's gradient to find a minimum.
Functions used to solve inhomogeneous differential equations subject to boundary conditions.
Twice-differentiable functions satisfying Laplace's equation.
The divergent sum of reciprocals of the positive integers.
Method for evaluating indeterminate limits by differentiating the numerator and denominator.
Representation of a complex function as a series including negative powers, used near singularities.
Standard way of assigning length, area, or volume to subsets of Euclidean space; the foundation of Lebesgue integration.
Point every neighborhood of which contains points of a set other than itself.
Value a function or sequence approaches as its input approaches some point.
Describes a function or sequence that is entirely non-increasing or non-decreasing.
Field concerned with finding the best solution, maximum or minimum, from a set of feasible options.
Describes a function that repeats its values at regular intervals.
Coefficients in a Laurent series capturing a complex function's behavior near a singularity.
Family of iterative methods for numerically approximating solutions to ordinary differential equations.
Property of a topological space containing a countable dense subset.
Ordered lists of numbers, often studied for convergence or patterns.
Operation adding a sequence of terms, often denoted with sigma notation.
Numerical integration method approximating area under a curve using trapezoids.
Assignment of a vector to every point in a space, studied via divergence and curl.
Values where a function equals zero, i.e. its roots.

sections.name: Geometry & Topology
sections.blurb: Shapes, spaces, curvature, and the properties preserved under continuous deformation.

entries.note: Topological property that every open cover has a finite subcover; equivalent to closed-and-bounded in Euclidean space.
Donut-shaped surface, a genus-1 example in topology, formed as the product of two circles.
Property of a set or function where the line segment between any two of its points stays within or above it.
19th-century German mathematician; developed Riemannian geometry, Riemann surfaces, the Riemann integral, and the Riemann hypothesis.
Topological property of a set containing all its limit points; the complement of an open set.
Number of independent directions or coordinates needed to describe a space.
Topological invariant V-E+F for polyhedra and surfaces, distinguishing shapes by genus.
Topological property of a space that cannot be split into two disjoint nonempty open sets.
Describes a set where any segment between two of its points lies entirely within it.
Embeddings of a circle in three-dimensional space studied up to continuous deformation; classified by invariants like the Jones polynomial.
German mathematician; namesake of the one-sided Mobius strip and of the Mobius function in number theory.
Set of points equidistant from a center; a basic conic section with circumference 2 pi r.
Measure of how a curve or surface deviates from being flat or straight.
Ancient Greek "father of geometry"; author of the Elements, systematizing geometry via axioms and postulates.
Self-similar geometric objects with fractional (non-integer) dimension, e.g. the Mandelbrot set.
Non-orientable surface with one side and one edge, formed by giving a strip a half-twist before joining its ends.
Describes a subset whose closure is the entire space, e.g. the rationals in the reals.
Spaces that locally resemble Euclidean space; central objects of differential geometry.
States every simply connected closed 3-manifold is homeomorphic to the 3-sphere; proved by Grigori Perelman.
Sets of points equidistant from a center point.
Ancient Greek mathematician and author of the Elements, foundational to axiomatic geometry.
Topological invariant counting the number of "holes" or handles in a surface.
Branch of mathematics concerning shapes, sizes, and properties of space.
French polymath and founder of algebraic topology; namesake of the Poincare conjecture.
Continuous deformation between two functions or paths; the basis of the fundamental group.
Conic section formed by the graph of a quadratic function; the locus of points equidistant from a focus and directrix.
States that in a right triangle, the square of the hypotenuse equals the sum of the squares of the other two sides.
Sets of points equidistant from a center in three or more dimensions.
Describes a space where every open cover has a finite subcover.
Measure of separation between two points, formalized via a metric.
German mathematician behind the Erlangen program classifying geometries by symmetry groups; namesake of the Klein bottle.
Describes a set not containing its boundary points; basic to topology's definition of a space.
One-dimensional complex manifolds allowing multivalued complex functions to be treated as single-valued.
Set of points equidistant from a center in three dimensions.
Tiling of a plane or space with shapes leaving no gaps or overlaps.
Branch of mathematics studying properties preserved under continuous deformation.
Three-sided polygons, subject of theorems like the Pythagorean theorem and the triangle inequality.
The boundary of a four-dimensional ball; the space at the center of the Poincare conjecture.
Curves — circle, ellipse, parabola, hyperbola — formed by intersecting a plane with a cone.
Smallest convex set containing a given set of points.
Number of coordinates needed to specify a point in a space.
Describes geometry with no parallel lines and positive curvature, or elliptic curves and functions in number theory.
Group capturing a topological space's loops up to continuous deformation, encoding basic shape information.
Continuous bijection with a continuous inverse, meaning two spaces are topologically equivalent.
Map from the 3-sphere to the 2-sphere with circular fibers, a foundational example in topology.
Describes non-Euclidean geometry with constant negative curvature, or hyperbolic trigonometric functions.
Non-orientable surface with no boundary that cannot be embedded in three dimensions without self-intersection.
Vector perpendicular to a surface or line at a given point.
Describes a surface with a consistent choice of "clockwise," unlike surfaces such as the Mobius strip.
Describes a polygon or polytope with all sides and angles equal, or a well-behaved point or space.
Continuous curves that pass through every point of a higher-dimensional region, e.g. the Hilbert curve.
Decomposition of a space into triangles or simplices, used in topology and computational geometry.

sections.name: Combinatorics & Graph Theory
sections.blurb: Counting, discrete structures, graphs, and the arrangements and colorings built from them.

entries.note: Orderings of a set's elements, counted by factorials and studied via symmetric groups.
Prolific, itinerant 20th-century Hungarian mathematician known for combinatorics and graph theory; namesake of the Erdos number.
Structures of vertices connected by edges, studied for connectivity, coloring, and paths.
Product of all positive integers up to n, denoted n!; counts the permutations of n objects.
States any planar map can be colored with four colors so no adjacent regions share a color; a landmark computer-assisted proof.
Integer sequence counting structures like balanced parentheses, polygon triangulations, and binary trees.
Describes structures with distinct, separate values, as opposed to continuous ones.
British mathematician who invented the Game of Life and surreal numbers, and contributed to group and knot theory.
Optimization technique maximizing or minimizing a linear objective subject to linear constraints.
Studies conditions under which order must appear within sufficiently large structures.
Numbers "n choose k" counting size-k subsets of an n-element set; the entries of Pascal's triangle.
Assignment of colors to graph vertices or edges so adjacent ones differ; related to the four-color theorem.
An ordered arrangement of a set's elements.
Grid-based computational systems evolving via local rules, e.g. Conway's Game of Life.
Minimum number of colors needed to color a graph so no adjacent vertices share a color.
Founder of information theory who defined entropy as a measure of information content.
Connections between vertices in a graph.
Product of all positive integers up to n, denoted n!.
States any planar map can be colored with four colors so adjacent regions differ.
Structure of vertices connected by edges.
British mathematician who invented the Game of Life and surreal numbers.
Von Neumann's result guaranteeing optimal strategies exist in two-player zero-sum games.
Subset of a graph's edges connecting all vertices with minimum total weight and no cycles.
Game-theoretic state where no player benefits from unilaterally changing strategy; named for John Nash.
Describes a graph that can be drawn on a plane without edges crossing.
Guarantees sufficiently large structures must contain an ordered substructure, e.g. a monochromatic clique.
Connected acyclic graphs, used in data structures and minimum spanning tree algorithms.

sections.name: Probability & Statistics
sections.blurb: Randomness, distributions, and the inferential methods used to analyze data.

entries.note: Measure of a distribution's spread around its mean; the square of the standard deviation.
Often refers to the bell-shaped Gaussian distribution, though also used for orthogonal vectors or subgroups.
The bell-shaped Gaussian distribution defined by mean and variance; central to the central limit theorem.
States sums of many independent random variables tend toward a normal distribution regardless of the original distribution.
Analysis of variance; statistical test comparing means across three or more groups.
The long-run average value of a random variable, weighted by probability.
Stochastic process where the next state depends only on the current state.
Statistical method modeling the relationship between a dependent variable and one or more predictors.
French mathematician and pioneer of measure theory and probability; namesake of Borel sets.
Probability of observing data as extreme as the sample under the null hypothesis; used in hypothesis testing.
Square root of the variance; measures spread of data around the mean.
Distribution where all outcomes in a range are equally likely.
Statistical measure of the linear relationship between two variables.
States sample averages converge to the expected value as sample size grows.
Stochastic processes where future states depend only on the present state.
Default assumption of no effect or difference, tested against an alternative in hypothesis testing.
Probability that a hypothesis test correctly rejects a false null hypothesis.
Distribution of a sum of squared standard normal variables, used in goodness-of-fit tests.
Distribution of the ratio of two scaled chi-squared variables, used in ANOVA.
Describes events or variables whose occurrence or value doesn't affect one another's probability.
Statistical method modeling a linear relationship between a dependent variable and predictors.
The average value of a data set, or the expected value of a distribution.
Data points that differ significantly from other observations in a data set.
Discrete distribution modeling the number of events in a fixed interval given a constant average rate.
Function describing the relative likelihood of a continuous random variable taking a given value.
Statistical technique estimating relationships between a dependent variable and predictors.
Differences between observed values and those predicted by a statistical model.
Bell-shaped distribution with heavier tails than normal, used for small-sample hypothesis testing.
18th-century English statistician and minister; namesake of Bayes' theorem relating conditional probabilities.
Russian mathematician; namesake of Markov chains modeling memoryless stochastic processes.
Formula relating conditional probabilities, updating a hypothesis's probability given new evidence.
Bounds the probability that a random variable deviates from its mean by more than a given amount, for any distribution.
Distribution where every value in an interval is equally likely.
Statistical technique partitioning data to assess a model's predictive performance.
Bar-chart representations of the distribution of numerical data.
Discrete distribution modeling draws without replacement from a finite population.
Swiss mathematician with foundational probability work including the law of large numbers; namesake of Bernoulli numbers and trials.
Bounds the probability that a nonnegative random variable exceeds a value, using only its mean.
Stochastic process whose expected future value equals its present value given past information.
Method estimating statistical model parameters by maximizing the likelihood of observed data.
The middle value of an ordered data set.
Russian mathematician; namesake of Chebyshev's inequality and Chebyshev polynomials.
Simeon Denis Poisson, namesake of the Poisson distribution modeling rare independent events.
Lack of pattern or predictability in events, formalized via probability theory.
Number of observations included in a statistical sample.
Process of selecting a subset of a population to estimate characteristics of the whole.
French mathematician; namesake of the Poisson distribution and Poisson's equation.
Statistical test comparing means, accounting for small sample sizes via the t-distribution.

sections.name: Numbers & Constants
sections.blurb: Specific numbers, constants, and numerals that recur as quizbowl answerlines.

entries.note: The additive identity; its historical development in India and Mesoamerica is a common quizbowl topic.
The multiplicative identity; a common numeric answerline.
Common small-integer answerline, e.g. the Platonic solids or quintic equations.
Common small-integer answerline, e.g. the four-color theorem or quaternions' four dimensions.
Ratio of a circle's circumference to its diameter, approximately 3.14159; a transcendental constant.
Common small-integer answerline, e.g. binary systems or quadratic equations.
A fixed numerical value in an equation or expression, as opposed to a variable.
Distance of a number from zero, always nonnegative.
Irrational constant (1+sqrt5)/2, approximately 1.618, with self-similar proportional properties linked to the Fibonacci sequence.
Describes a number greater than zero.
Greek letter used to denote summation or, in lowercase, standard deviation.
Common small-integer answerline, e.g. months in a year or faces of a dodecahedron.
Common small-integer answerline; the smallest perfect number.
Greek letter commonly denoting eigenvalues or the parameter of the Poisson and exponential distributions.
Common fractional answerline, e.g. the real part of the Riemann hypothesis's zeros.
Common small-integer answerline.
Common variable in dynamical systems and differential equations, treated as an independent variable.
